text: This video depicts a robotic excision of left adrenal hemorrhagic cyst in a pregnant lady. A twenty-four year old lady had presented with nausea and vomiting. She was found to have an eight point seven centimeter adrenal cyst and she was eight weeks pregnant. An EUSN biopsy did not show any malignant cells. CTA and MRI confirmed it to be a hemorrhagic cyst. Because of persistent pain, she was taken to the operating room eight weeks later when she was seventeen weeks pregnant.

The surgeon enters the abdomen using an optical trocar and inserts robotic trocars under visual guidance. The splenic flexure is mobilized and dissected from the kidney and spleen. An intraoperative ultrasound is performed to assess vascularity, revealing no active bleeding and clear planes between the renal vessels and aorta. Using a vessel sealer, tissues around the adrenal cyst are dissected inferiorly and laterally, followed by final dissection with scissors. The cyst is dissected from the inferior, median, and superior aspects, identifying the adrenal gland on the superior lateral margin. A small feeding vessel to the spleen is ligated.

The surgeon continues dissection on the lateral and inferior aspects of the edematous spleen, aiding in better mobilization. Superior dissection between the pancreas allows further mobilization of the spleen and improved access to the cyst. On the medial aspect, the plane between the adrenal gland and cyst is developed, separating them from major vessels. The superior pole is taken with a vessel sealer, encountering and controlling bleeding with bipolar cautery. The dissection continues from the medial aspect, controlling bleeding from the cyst with bipolar cautery. The superior pole is mobilized from the medial and superior aspects of the adrenal gland. Medial dissection continues, taking all feeding vessels, and a large adrenal vein leading to the renal vessel is dissected. Posterior attachments are taken with hot scissors.

The surgeon places locking clips on the adrenal vein and ligates it using a vessel sealer. The posterior attachments are dissected with the vessel sealer. The cyst is placed in an endocatch bag and extracted through a small midline incision.
